title: Work Budgeting
use_for: preventing burst-heavy execution on critical threads
choosing overflow strategies for excess work
shaping latency versus throughput through bounded work
turning unbounded update loops into budgeted execution
do_not_use_for: generic profiling without a budget decision to make
UI architecture as a primary owner
rules: every critical phase should have an explicit work budget
overflow behavior must be defined before load arrives
prefer bounded carry-over state over hidden burst paths
budget policy should reflect latency versus throughput goals explicitly
measure worst-case spikes, not averages alone
procedure: Name the runtime phase that needs a budget.
Define the maximum allowed work or time per pass.
Choose overflow behavior: queue, defer, merge, or drop.
Measure worst-case latency and backlog growth under load.
Adjust chunk size, overflow policy, or phase boundaries.

primary_when: the request is specifically about frame budgets, phase budgets, bounded work, backlog control, or overflow strategy
the request is about making a runtime phase responsive by enforcing explicit work limits
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about feature-domain behavior with no budgeting decision
the request is about raw data layout or cache locality without phase budgeting concerns

solves: how much work a runtime phase is allowed to do
what should happen when demand exceeds the allowed budget
how to keep responsiveness stable under fluctuating load

core_principles: Unbounded work on critical phases becomes the real bottleneck even when average cost looks acceptable.
A budget is incomplete until overflow behavior is explicit.
Latency and throughput priorities should shape the budget instead of emerging accidentally.
constraints: Budget checks must cost less than the spike behavior they prevent.
Overflow policy must preserve correctness for the owning domain.
Backlog growth must remain observable instead of accumulating silently.
decision_rules: Use work-budgeting as the owner when the central design question is how much work a phase may perform and what happens to overflow.
If responsiveness matters more than batch efficiency, prefer smaller bounded slices with explicit carry-over.
If batching improves throughput materially, allow larger windows only within an explicit worst-case latency budget.
If overflow is unbounded or undefined, the budget model is incomplete regardless of average performance.

preferred_patterns: bounded chunk processing on critical threads
overflow policies chosen per runtime phase
avoid_patterns: unbounded update loops on the main thread
budget numbers that exist only in comments or intuition
throughput-oriented batching with no worst-case latency guardrail
silent backlog accumulation with no visibility

recipes.name: Budget a critical runtime phase
recipes.when: Use when a frame, tick, or update phase risks doing too much work at once.
steps: Define the maximum allowed work or time for the phase.
Split the workload into bounded units with carry-over state.
Choose and document the overflow policy.
Measure worst-case latency, backlog depth, and visible spikes under load.

pitfalls: Defining a budget without deciding what happens when it is exceeded.
Tuning for average throughput while ignoring worst-case spikes.
Using tiny slices that drown the system in queue overhead.
review_checklist: Does the critical phase have an explicit budget?
Is overflow behavior defined and observable?
Is the budget aligned with latency versus throughput goals?
Are worst-case spikes measured instead of averages alone?

references.title: Local Reference: Work Budgeting
references.interpretation: Use this first for phase budget design, overflow strategies, and spike prevention policy.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for bounded work and backlog control.

references.title: Performance Domain Foundations
references.interpretation: Use this to align budgets with the broader performance doctrine on latency, throughput, and lifecycle control.
references.why: Use to keep budgeting decisions consistent with the domain's cost model.
